citations.short_citation: World Health Organization. Yellow fever fact sheet.
citations.claim_supported: Supports yellow fever transmission geography, endemic risk, and vaccination framing.
citations.note: Verified public source; not a Wikipedia or DOI evidence link.

geography_layers.label: Tropical Africa and South America mosquito-endemic realm
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary yellow fever endemic and vaccination-risk geography
geography_layers.narrative: Yellow fever is mapped as a tropical Africa and South America mosquito-endemic realm, a schematic layer for enzootic and outbreak risk rather than a hard administrative boundary.

atlas.why_it_matters: Yellow fever moves with people, water storage, ships, and vector habitat, not by human travel alone.

citations.short_citation: World Health Organization. Cholera fact sheet.
citations.claim_supported: Supports cholera water, sanitation, outbreak, and endemic-risk geography.
citations.note: Verified public source; not a Wikipedia or DOI evidence link.

geography_layers.label: Water, sanitation, outbreak, and endemic-risk belt
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary cholera outbreak and endemic-risk geography
geography_layers.narrative: Cholera is represented as a broad belt of water, sanitation, displacement, conflict, and outbreak risk; the main spatial signal is infrastructure vulnerability rather than a precise pathogen range.

citations.short_citation: World Health Organization. Measles fact sheet.
citations.claim_supported: Supports measles global transmission and vaccination-gap geography.
citations.note: Verified public source; not a Wikipedia or DOI evidence link.

geography_layers.label: Global airborne and vaccination-gap distribution
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary global measles elimination and resurgence geography
geography_layers.narrative: Measles maps globally through airborne transmission and immunity gaps, with risk shaped by vaccine coverage, importation, crowding, and surveillance rather than a natural endemic frontier.

atlas.summary: Measles has a partly reconstructed deeper origin story from molecular-clock work, while its colonial spread is brutally visible in the Americas and Pacific.

citations.short_citation: World Health Organization. Mpox fact sheet.
citations.claim_supported: Supports mpox zoonotic endemic geography and global outbreak framing.
citations.note: Verified public source; not a Wikipedia or DOI evidence link.

geography_layers.label: Central and West African zoonotic endemic interface with global outbreak routes
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary zoonotic endemic and international outbreak geography
geography_layers.narrative: Mpox is mapped as a Central and West African zoonotic endemic interface, with global outbreak spread treated as networked transmission layered on top of reservoir ecology and surveillance gaps.

atlas.summary: Mpox combines a geographically anchored Central and West African history with wildlife-trade export events and a distinct twenty-first-century pattern of networked global transmission.
atlas.why_it_matters: Mpox geography separates clades, transport events, animal reservoirs, and transmission networks instead of collapsing them into a single point of origin.

geography_layers.label: Global avian migration, poultry, dairy, and mammal interface
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary global avian-influenza panzootic and spillover geography
geography_layers.narrative: H5N1 is mapped as a global animal-interface pattern, where migratory birds, poultry systems, mammal spillover, dairy herds, culling, and occupational exposure define the risk surface.

atlas.summary: H5N1 is not primarily a human epidemic map. Its geography follows bird migration, poultry systems, mammal spillover, dairy-herd events, and occupational exposure.
atlas.why_it_matters: The spatial problem is One Health risk: animal movement, farm systems, and spillover opportunities before sustained human-to-human transmission.

citations.short_citation: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. About hantavirus.
citations.claim_supported: Supports rodent reservoir and exposure geography for hantaviruses.
citations.note: Verified public source; not a Wikipedia or DOI evidence link.

geography_layers.label: Global rodent-reservoir and aerosol-exposure distribution
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary global rodent-reservoir and spillover geography
geography_layers.narrative: Hantaviruses are mapped as a global rodent-reservoir and aerosolized-excreta exposure layer, with regional syndromes and named viruses nested inside local rodent ecology and housing or occupational exposure.

atlas.why_it_matters: Hantavirus geography depends on host ecology, housing, work exposures, and syndrome differences across regions, not a single route line.
atlas.summary: Hantavirus geography follows rodent-host biogeography, ecological exposure settings, and the split between Old World hemorrhagic-fever viruses and New World cardiopulmonary viruses.

citations.short_citation: World Health Organization. Dengue and severe dengue fact sheet.
citations.claim_supported: Supports dengue tropical/subtropical Aedes distribution and urban outbreak geography.
citations.note: Verified public source; not a Wikipedia or DOI evidence link.

geography_layers.label: Tropical and subtropical Aedes urban-transmission belt
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary dengue expansion and urban arboviral geography
geography_layers.narrative: Dengue is mapped as a tropical and subtropical Aedes urban-transmission belt, where climate, urban water storage, vector range, mobility, and surveillance shape outbreak geography.

atlas.why_it_matters: Dengue shows how urbanization, climate, vector range, water storage, travel, and surveillance reshape disease geography in real time.

citations.short_citation: World Health Organization. Malaria fact sheet.
citations.claim_supported: Supports malaria endemic geography and vector-control framing.
citations.note: Verified public source; not a Wikipedia or DOI evidence link.

geography_layers.label: Tropical and subtropical Anopheles transmission belt
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary malaria endemic, elimination, and vector-control geography
geography_layers.narrative: Malaria is mapped as a tropical and subtropical Anopheles transmission belt, with local intensity determined by mosquito ecology, housing, control programs, drug resistance, climate, and health-system reach.

atlas.summary: Malaria shows how vector ecology, land use, labor systems, altitude, war, and climate can redraw disease geography across centuries without a single tidy origin story.
atlas.why_it_matters: Its geography is built around mosquitoes, parasite species, ecological receptivity, control programs, and uneven health-system reach.

geography_layers.label: Global airborne, crowding, HIV, migration, and resistance distribution
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary global tuberculosis burden and drug-resistance geography
geography_layers.narrative: Tuberculosis is mapped globally through airborne transmission, crowding, poverty, HIV, migration, incarceration, treatment completion, and drug resistance rather than a bounded endemic region.

atlas.summary: Tuberculosis is ancient and modern at once: a human co-traveler that thrives in crowding, poverty, institutional confinement, migration, HIV, and uneven public-health capacity.
atlas.why_it_matters: Its map is chronic and structural: airborne transmission shaped by housing, labor, prisons, care access, and drug-resistance systems.

citations.short_citation: World Health Organization. Plague fact sheet.
citations.claim_supported: Supports plague reservoir ecology and contemporary endemic foci framing.
citations.note: Verified public source; not a Wikipedia or DOI evidence link.

geography_layers.label: Global rodent-reservoir foci and historical port-spread surface
geography_layers.date_or_era: Contemporary reservoir ecology with historical pandemic route memory
geography_layers.narrative: Plague is mapped as a global but focal rodent-reservoir ecology layer, with historical port and overland routes layered onto enzootic foci rather than treated as a purely medieval or purely maritime disease.

atlas.summary: Plague is one of the clearest historical demonstrations that reservoir ecology, trade corridors, warfare, and maritime ports can repeatedly rewire a continent.
atlas.why_it_matters: Plague links deep historical fear, genomic reconstruction, and the practical geography of rodents, fleas, and ports.
